lesson.menu_title: The Client Conference — Advising on the Deal
lesson.menu_desc: Reporting & advisory verb patterns (advise / recommend / suggest / warn / urge -- to + verb vs gerund vs that-clause) for advising the client and managing expectations -- 29 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 18 -- The Client Conference: sitting with your client, advising on the deal, breaking bad news, and managing expectations.
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Luiz Bressane | Aula 18 -- The Client Conference

listenings.text: The hardest part of a defender's job is not the courtroom; it is the room where you sit alone with your client and tell them the truth. When you advise a client on a deal, three rules matter. First, be candid. Do not soften the outlook so much that they cannot make a real choice; a client who is not given the full picture cannot truly consent. Second, manage expectations from the very first meeting. Many clients arrive believing they will walk free, so I always recommend setting a realistic prospect early, before hope hardens into a demand. Third, advise, but never decide for them. I urge my clients to weigh every drawback themselves; I warn them against rushing, and I make sure they understand each caveat. In the end, the choice is theirs. I suggest they take the night to think, and I am upfront about every trade-off. A good defender does not just win cases; they prepare a client to face the truth.

You are listening to the legal affairs review, with a feature this week on the lawyer-client relationship. Studies of public defenders suggest that the quality of advice given in private conferences shapes outcomes as much as the trial itself. Experts recommend that defenders be candid about the likely prospect of each option, even when the news is hard to hear. One veteran defender we spoke to said she always warns her clients against accepting the first offer too quickly, and urges them to consider every implication before they sign. She also advises colleagues to be upfront about the drawbacks of a deal, and recommends giving the client time rather than pressing for an immediate answer. The message from the panel was clear: managing expectations and giving honest, realistic counsel is not a courtesy. It is a duty.
